text: HIRING YOUR FIRST EMPLOYEE — WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW. Knowing when to hire is crucial: if you are regularly turning away work, missing deadlines, or your business cannot grow because you are doing everything alone, it is time to hire. Under the Uganda Employment Act 2006, every employer must provide a written employment contract. The contract must include: names of both parties, job title and description, start date, probation period (maximum 6 months), wages and pay frequency, working hours, leave entitlement, and notice period for termination. Key legal requirements: Minimum employment age is 18 for formal employment (16 for light work with guardian consent). Maximum working hours: 48 hours per week (8 hours/day for 6 days). Overtime must be voluntary and compensated at 1.5x the normal hourly rate. Probation period cannot exceed 6 months — after that, the employee must be confirmed or terminated with proper notice. Payroll deductions you must make: NSSF contributions (15% total — 10% employer, 5% employee), PAYE (Pay As You Earn) income tax based on tax brackets, and Local Service Tax (LST) where applicable. Register with NSSF and URA before your first employee starts. Pro tip: Start with one part-time employee or apprentice to test the arrangement before committing to full-time hiring.

PAYING EMPLOYEES LEGALLY IN UGANDA. Wages: Uganda has no formal minimum wage for the private sector (the last gazetted minimum wage of UGX 6,000/month dates from 1984 and is effectively obsolete). The public sector baseline is approximately UGX 130,000/month. In practice, fair market wages depend on the role and location — negotiate fairly based on the work and local cost of living. Example wages: shop attendant UGX 200,000-400,000/month, skilled worker (mechanic, tailor) UGX 300,000-600,000/month, delivery rider UGX 250,000-400,000/month. Pay frequency: Monthly is standard, but weekly or bi-weekly is acceptable if stated in the contract. NSSF registration: Mandatory for employers with 5 or more employees. Employer pays 10% of gross salary, employee pays 5% (deducted from wages). Remit by the 15th of the following month. PAYE withholding: Based on individual income tax brackets. No PAYE required on monthly wages below UGX 235,000. Above that, withhold according to the progressive rates (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%). File monthly PAYE returns on URA's e-filing portal by the 15th of the following month. Overtime: Paid at 1.5x normal rate for hours beyond 48/week. Work on public holidays: 2x normal rate. Annual leave: Minimum 21 working days per year (after 12 months of continuous service). Sick leave: Up to 1 month on full pay, 1 month on half pay per 12-month period, with medical certificate. Maternity leave: 60 working days on full pay for female employees.

TERMINATION AND EMPLOYEE RIGHTS. Ending an employment relationship must follow the Uganda Employment Act 2006 to avoid legal liability. Notice periods (based on length of service): Less than 6 months of service — 2 weeks notice. 6 months to 1 year — 1 month notice. 1 to 5 years — 2 months notice. 5 to 10 years — 3 months notice. Over 10 years — 4 months notice. The employer can pay wages in lieu of the notice period instead of requiring the employee to work through it. Severance pay: Negotiated between employer and employee or as specified in the employment contract. There is no statutory formula, but common practice is 1 month's pay per year of service. Unfair dismissal: An employee who believes they were unfairly dismissed can file a complaint with the Labour Officer or sue at the Industrial Court. Grounds for unfair dismissal include: dismissal without valid reason, dismissal without following proper procedure, dismissal based on discrimination (gender, pregnancy, disability, union membership). Redundancy: When reducing staff for economic reasons, the last-in-first-out (LIFO) principle applies unless there are objective criteria for selection. The employer must consult with the Labour Officer and give reasonable notice. Final pay: All outstanding wages, accrued leave pay, and any severance must be paid within 7 days of the termination date. Failure to pay on time can result in additional penalties at the Industrial Court. Keep written records of all employment decisions, warnings, and performance reviews — they are your evidence in case of a dispute.
